one_line_takeaway: RAILS shows that a gradient-free, prior-free random local search -- using only an auto-regressive prefix-matching loss and history-based (exploit+explore) candidate selection -- matches or beats gradient-based (GCG) and template/agent-based (AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP) jailbreak baselines on open models and, via cross-tokenizer ensemble optimization, transfers better to GPT-3.5/GPT-4/Gemini Pro than transfer baselines that even use GPT-4 as their attack source.
key_finding: RAILS matches or exceeds gradient-based attacks while removing both the white-box and handcrafted-prior requirements. Table 1 (best ASR, %): RAILS reaches 90.2 on Llama2 (vs GCG 43.9, AutoDAN 2.4, PAIR 9.8, TAP 7.3, BEAST 0.0, PRS 82.0), 92.7 on Llama3 (vs GCG 9.0, PRS 95.0), and 100.0 on Vicuna, Qwen, and Baichuan2. Table 2 (transfer ASR, %): RAILS-T reaches 82.9 on GPT-3.5 Turbo (vs GCG-T 53.5, TAP-T* 60.0), 22.0 on GPT-4 Turbo (vs GCG-T 19.5), and 51.2 on Gemini Pro (vs GCG-T 12.5, TAP-T* 40.0) -- notably outperforming TAP-T even though TAP-T uses GPT-4 Turbo itself as its attack source while RAILS-T uses only open-source models. Ablation (Table 3, Llama2, equal compute, 20-token suffix): the core local-search+LAR loss alone reaches 42+/-4% ASR (comparable to GCG's 45+/-5%), but adding history-based selection and the few-shot strategy raises this to 80+/-2%, and removing LAR (keeping history+few-shot) drops it to 77+/-1%. Cross-tokenizer diversity drives transfer: adding Llama3 to the source ensemble raised GPT-4 transfer ASR from 12.2% (single/pair sources) to 22.0% (Table S2/Fig. 3). Suffix-length trade-off: longer suffixes (40-60 tokens) maximize source-model ASR but shorter suffixes (5-20 tokens) transfer better to black-box targets. Selection-strategy ablation: the hybrid exploit/explore selection boosts validation success from ~78% (greedy Best-100) to 86%. Tokenizer analysis: mean pairwise vocabulary overlap across evaluated models is only 49.0%, yet multi-model-optimized suffixes retain 84.0% token consistency across tokenizers vs. 81.1% for single-model-optimized suffixes. Under a strict perplexity filter (5% false-positive rate on real ShareGPT prompts), RAILS maintains 38% ASR on Llama2 vs. 14% for GCG and 0% for BEAST. Efficiency: first successful jailbreak converges in as few as 12+/-8 iterations on Vicuna and 173+/-145 on Llama2 (Table 5). Qualitative analysis identifies six recurring discovered attack primitives (role-playing, instruction/context manipulation, code injection, technical jargon, formatting disruption, linguistic obfuscation), with formatting and instruction/context manipulation most prevalent; closed models like GPT-4 were found more vulnerable to cybercrime/intrusion-category transfer prompts than to harassment/general-harm prompts. A worked case study shows RAILS-T-optimized suffixes eliciting a working SQL-injection/DNS-exfiltration payload from GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and Gemini Pro (Llama2 alone refused in that example).
core_contribution: The paper introduces RAILS (RAndom Iterative Local Search), a gradient-free, prior-free jailbreak optimization framework that operates using only a target LLM's output logits (gray-box access). Its two novel components are: (1) an auto-regressive loss (L_AR) that penalizes a candidate suffix with a large constant C the moment the model's greedy generation deviates from the target affirmative prefix, forcing exact-prefix-first optimization (combined with the standard teacher-forcing loss via a convex combination L = alpha*L_AR + (1-alpha)*L_TF, alpha=0.9); and (2) a history-based candidate selection strategy that, after the iterative local-search loop, samples K=100 candidates from the full search history using a hybrid of exploitation (lowest loss) and exploration (temporal diversity across iterations) rather than greedily taking the single lowest-loss candidate, then validates/filters them with a classifier before a few-shot attack on the target. Because perturbation generation is decoupled from gradient computation, RAILS also supports a cross-tokenizer ensemble attack: candidates are scored against multiple source models with different tokenizers simultaneously (weighted loss sum, weights adapted toward not-yet-broken models), which the paper argues discovers adversarial patterns that transfer across disjoint vocabularies.
threat_model: Gray-box: the attacker has query access to a target LLM and can observe output logits/token probabilities but has no access to model weights or gradients (explicitly distinguished from white-box gradient attacks like GCG). Attack goal: append an adversarial suffix of length L to a harmful user query so that the model both begins its response with an affirmative target prefix (proxy objective, optimized) and produces a full response judged harmful by a classifier (true objective, evaluated separately). For transfer attacks, the attacker optimizes against an open-source ensemble (e.g., Llama2 + Vicuna + Qwen) and tests transfer to black-box closed-source targets (GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4 Turbo, Gemini Pro 1.5) with no query access to those targets during optimization.
limitations: Authors state (Discussion/Limitations section): (1) RAILS remains computationally expensive, requiring a large number of model queries per attack, despite being more query-efficient than training-based methods; (2) the study evaluates only model-level defenses and does not assess system-level defenses such as auxiliary input/output filtering or moderation APIs; (3) RAILS finds effective adversarial prompts but does not explain why certain token perturbations generalize better than others -- interpretability of the discovered vulnerabilities is left as future work.

datasets_used: ShareGPT (source of real-user-prompt perplexity distribution for the stealthiness evaluation)
quantitative_results: Direct ASR (Table 1): RAILS 90.2% Llama2, 92.7% Llama3, 100.0% Vicuna, 100.0% Qwen, 100.0% Baichuan2
Transfer ASR (Table 2): RAILS-T 82.9% GPT-3.5 Turbo, 22.0% GPT-4 Turbo, 51.2% Gemini Pro
Ablation ASR on Llama2, equal compute (Table 3): RAILS full 80+/-2%; w/o few-shot 78+/-3%; w/o LAR 77+/-1%; RAILS Core (no history/few-shot) 42+/-4%; GCG 45+/-5%
Ensemble diversity effect on GPT-4 transfer (Table S2/Fig.3): 12.2% (single/pair source) to 22.0% (Llama3+Vicuna+Qwen source)
Mean pairwise tokenizer vocabulary overlap: 49.0%; suffix token consistency: 84.0% (multi-model) vs 81.1% (single-model)
Perplexity-filter robustness at 5% false-positive rate on real prompts: RAILS 38% ASR vs GCG 14% vs BEAST 0%
First-success iteration count (Table 5): Vicuna 12+/-8, Qwen 22+/-19, Llama3 61+/-46, Llama2 173+/-145
Selection-strategy ablation: hybrid selection raises validation success from ~78% (greedy) to 86%
